definition_question: What is AI?
Artificial intelligence is the field of creating machines that can perform tasks requiring human intelligence, such as learning, reasoning, and problem-solving.
What is machine learning?
Machine learning is a branch of AI where systems learn patterns from data instead of being explicitly programmed, improving through experience.
What is deep learning?
Deep learning uses multi-layer neural networks to learn hierarchical representations of data, enabling advances in image recognition, NLP, and more.
What is a neural network?
A neural network is a computing system inspired by biological brains, composed of layers of interconnected nodes (neurons) that process information.
What is a transformer?
A transformer is a neural network architecture that uses self-attention to process sequences in parallel, forming the basis of modern LLMs.
What is an attention mechanism?
Attention is a mechanism that allows a model to focus on relevant parts of the input when producing each part of the output, weighing importance dynamically.
What is a language model?
A language model is a statistical model that predicts the probability of a sequence of words, enabling text generation and understanding.
What is natural language processing?
Natural language processing (NLP) is a field of AI focused on enabling computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language.
What is computer vision?
Computer vision is a field of AI that trains computers to interpret and understand visual information from images or video.
What is reinforcement learning?
Reinforcement learning trains agents through trial and error, using rewards and punishments to learn optimal behavior.
What is supervised learning?
Supervised learning trains models on labeled data where input-output pairs are provided, learning to map inputs to outputs.
What is unsupervised learning?
Unsupervised learning finds patterns in unlabeled data without predefined outputs or labels.
What is semi-supervised learning?
Semi-supervised learning combines a small amount of labeled data with a large amount of unlabeled data during training.
What is self-supervised learning?
Self-supervised learning generates labels from the data itself, such as predicting masked words or the next frame in a video.
What is transfer learning?
Transfer learning takes a pre-trained model and fine-tunes it on a related but different task, saving training time.
What is few-shot learning?
Few-shot learning trains models to generalize from very few examples, often using meta-learning or in-context learning.
What is zero-shot learning?
Zero-shot learning enables a model to perform tasks it was never explicitly trained on by leveraging related knowledge.
What is an embedding?
An embedding is a dense vector representation of data (words, images, etc.) that captures semantic meaning in a continuous space.
What is gradient descent?
Gradient descent is an optimization algorithm that iteratively moves parameters in the direction that reduces the loss function.
What is stochastic gradient descent?
Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) updates parameters using one random sample at a time, making it faster and allowing escape from local minima.
What is backpropagation?
Backpropagation computes gradients by applying the chain rule through the network from output back to input, enabling weight updates during training.
What is a loss function?
A loss function measures how wrong a model's predictions are compared to the true values — lower loss means better predictions.
What is cross-entropy loss?
Cross-entropy loss measures the difference between two probability distributions, commonly used for classification tasks.
What is mean squared error?
Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average squared difference between predicted and actual values, used in regression tasks.
What is overfitting?
Overfitting occurs when a model memorizes training data instead of learning general patterns, performing well on training but poorly on new data.
What is underfitting?
Underfitting occurs when a model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns in the data, resulting in poor performance on both training and test sets.
What is regularization?
Regularization is a technique to prevent overfitting by adding a penalty for complex models, encouraging simpler, more general solutions.
What is L1 regularization?
L1 regularization (Lasso) adds the absolute value of weights as a penalty, encouraging sparse models with many zero weights.
What is L2 regularization?
L2 regularization (Ridge) adds the squared magnitude of weights as a penalty, encouraging small but non-zero weights.
What is dropout?
Dropout is a regularization technique that randomly deactivates a fraction of neurons during training to prevent overfitting.
What is batch normalization?
Batch normalization normalizes layer inputs across a mini-batch, stabilizing training and allowing higher learning rates.
What is layer normalization?
Layer normalization normalizes inputs across the feature dimension for each sample, commonly used in transformers.
What is an activation function?
An activation function introduces non-linearity into neural networks, allowing them to learn complex patterns.
What is ReLU?
ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) outputs the input if positive, zero otherwise, and is the most commonly used activation function.
What is a softmax function?
Softmax converts a vector of numbers into a probability distribution, used for multi-class classification.
What is a convolutional neural network?
A CNN is a neural network that uses convolution operations to process grid-like data, particularly effective for images.
What is a recurrent neural network?
An RNN is a neural network designed for sequential data that maintains a hidden state to capture temporal dependencies.
What is an LSTM?
LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) is an RNN variant with gating mechanisms that can learn long-range dependencies without vanishing gradients.
What is a GAN?
A Generative Adversarial Network pits two networks (generator and discriminator) against each other to generate realistic synthetic data.
What is a variational autoencoder?
A VAE is a generative model that learns a latent representation of data and can generate new samples by decoding from the latent space.
What is a diffusion model?
A diffusion model generates data by gradually denoising random noise through a learned reverse process.
What is an autoencoder?
An autoencoder learns to compress data into a lower-dimensional representation and then reconstruct it, useful for dimensionality reduction.
What is Python?
Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language known for its readability, versatility, and extensive ecosystem of libraries.
What is PyTorch?
PyTorch is an open-source machine learning framework developed by Meta that provides tensor computation and automatic differentiation.
What is TensorFlow?
TensorFlow is an open-source machine learning platform developed by Google for building and deploying ML models.
What is JAX?
JAX is a library for high-performance numerical computing with automatic differentiation and JIT compilation via XLA.
What is NumPy?
NumPy is a Python library for numerical computing that provides multidimensional arrays and linear algebra operations.
What is an algorithm?
An algorithm is a step-by-step procedure for solving a problem or accomplishing a task, like a recipe for computation.
What is a data structure?
A data structure is a way of organizing and storing data for efficient access and modification.
What is an array?
An array is a collection of elements stored at contiguous memory locations, accessible by index.
What is a linked list?
A linked list is a linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes that point to the next node.
What is a hash table?
A hash table maps keys to values using a hash function for O(1) average lookup time.
What is a stack?
A stack is a last-in-first-out (LIFO) data structure where elements are added and removed from the top.
What is a queue?
A queue is a first-in-first-out (FIFO) data structure where elements are added at the back and removed from the front.
What is a tree?
A tree is a hierarchical data structure consisting of nodes connected by edges, with a root node and child nodes.
What is a graph?
A graph is a data structure consisting of nodes (vertices) connected by edges, representing relationships.
What is recursion?
Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve a problem by breaking it into smaller subproblems.
What is dynamic programming?
Dynamic programming solves complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results to avoid recomputation.
What is time complexity?
Time complexity measures the amount of time an algorithm takes as a function of input size, expressed using Big O notation.
What is space complexity?
Space complexity measures the amount of memory an algorithm uses as a function of input size.
What is Big O notation?
Big O notation describes the upper bound of an algorithm's growth rate, classifying efficiency as O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n²), etc.
What is object-oriented programming?
OOP is a programming paradigm based on objects containing data and methods, with principles like encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism.
What is functional programming?
Functional programming is a paradigm that treats computation as the evaluation of mathematical functions, avoiding mutable state.
What is a compiler?
A compiler translates source code written in a high-level programming language into machine code that a computer can execute.
What is an interpreter?
An interpreter directly executes source code line by line without a separate compilation step, like Python or JavaScript.
What is an API?
An API (Application Programming Interface) is a set of rules that lets different software applications communicate and exchange data.
What is REST?
REST is an architectural style for APIs that uses standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to operate on resources.
What is GraphQL?
GraphQL is a query language for APIs that lets clients request exactly the data they need, reducing over-fetching.
What is JSON?
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight data interchange format that is human-readable and easy for machines to parse.
What is SQL?
SQL (Structured Query Language) is used to manage and query relational databases.
What is NoSQL?
NoSQL databases are non-relational data stores designed for scalability, flexibility, and handling unstructured data.
What is a database?
A database is an organized collection of structured data, typically stored and accessed electronically via a database management system.
What is a relational database?
A relational database organizes data into tables with rows and columns, linked by relationships and queried via SQL.
What is a server?
A server is a computer or system that provides resources, data, services, or functionality to other computers (clients) over a network.
What is cloud computing?
Cloud computing delivers computing services (servers, storage, databases, networking) over the internet on a pay-as-you-go basis.
What is Docker?
Docker is a platform for running applications in isolated containers — lightweight, portable, and consistent across environments.
What is Kubernetes?
Kubernetes is an orchestration platform for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications.
What is Linux?
Linux is a free, open-source operating system kernel that powers most servers, Android phones, and embedded devices.
What is GitHub?
GitHub is a platform for version control and collaboration using Git. It hosts source code and enables team development.
What is Git?
Git is a distributed version control system that tracks changes in source code during software development.
What is an operating system?
An operating system (OS) is system software that manages computer hardware, software resources, and provides common services for programs.
What is a GPU?
A Graphics Processing Unit (GPU) is a specialized processor designed for parallel computation, essential for training neural networks.
What is a CPU?
A Central Processing Unit (CPU) is the primary processor in a computer that executes instructions sequentially, handling general-purpose tasks.
What is an FPGA?
A Field-Programmable Gate Array is an integrated circuit that can be configured after manufacturing for specific computing tasks.
What is HTTP?
HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is the foundation protocol for data communication on the World Wide Web.
What is HTTPS?
HTTPS is HTTP with encryption via TLS/SSL, ensuring secure communication between a browser and a web server.
What is the internet?
The internet is a global network of interconnected computers that communicate via standardized protocols to share information.
What is a protocol?
A protocol is a set of rules governing data communication between devices on a network.
What is encryption?
Encryption is the process of converting information into a coded form to prevent unauthorized access.
What is cybersecurity?
Cybersecurity is the practice of protecting systems, networks, and data from digital attacks, theft, and damage.
What is data science?
Data science combines statistics, programming, and domain expertise to extract insights and knowledge from data.
What is entropy?
Entropy is a measure of disorder or randomness in a system. In thermodynamics, it quantifies unavailable energy; in information theory, it measures uncertainty.
What is gravity?
Gravity is a natural force that attracts objects with mass toward one another. On Earth, it gives us weight and keeps us grounded.
What is a black hole?
A black hole is a region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape it.
What is a neutron star?
A neutron star is the collapsed core of a massive star, composed almost entirely of neutrons and extremely dense.
What is light?
Light is electromagnetic radiation that travels as waves. It is visible to the human eye and essential for vision.
What is energy?
Energy is the capacity to do work. It exists in many forms including kinetic, potential, thermal, chemical, and nuclear.
What is matter?
Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space. It is composed of atoms and molecules.
What is an atom?
An atom is the basic unit of matter, consisting of a nucleus of protons and neutrons surrounded by electrons.
What is a molecule?
A molecule is a group of two or more atoms bonded together, representing the smallest unit of a chemical compound.
What is quantum mechanics?
Quantum mechanics is the branch of physics that describes the behavior of matter and energy at atomic and subatomic scales.
What is a quantum?
A quantum is the smallest discrete unit of a physical quantity, such as energy or angular momentum.
What is wave-particle duality?
Wave-particle duality is the concept that quantum objects exhibit both wave-like and particle-like properties depending on how they are measured.
What is the uncertainty principle?
Heisenberg's uncertainty principle states that certain pairs of physical properties cannot be simultaneously known with arbitrary precision.
What is quantum entanglement?
Quantum entanglement is a phenomenon where two particles become correlated such that measuring one instantly affects the other, regardless of distance.
What is superposition?
Superposition is the principle that a quantum system can exist in multiple states simultaneously until measured.
What is relativity?
Relativity is Einstein's theory describing how spacetime is curved by mass and energy, and how motion affects measurements of time and space.
What is special relativity?
Special relativity describes how space and time are relative to the observer's motion, with the speed of light as a universal constant.
What is general relativity?
General relativity describes gravity as the curvature of spacetime caused by mass and energy.
What is the speed of light?
The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 299,792,458 meters per second (about 300,000 km/s).
What is electromagnetism?
Electromagnetism is the branch of physics studying the electromagnetic force between electrically charged particles.
What is an electromagnetic wave?
An electromagnetic wave is a wave of electric and magnetic fields that travels through space, including light, radio waves, and X-rays.
What is the electromagnetic spectrum?
The electromagnetic spectrum spans all frequencies of electromagnetic radiation from radio waves to gamma rays.
What is thermodynamics?
Thermodynamics is the branch of physics dealing with heat, work, temperature, and the relationships between them.
What is the first law of thermodynamics?
The first law states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another.
What is the second law of thermodynamics?
The second law states that the entropy of an isolated system always increases over time.
What is entropy in information theory?
In information theory, entropy quantifies the amount of uncertainty or information content in a random variable.
What is nuclear fission?
Nuclear fission is the splitting of a heavy atomic nucleus into lighter nuclei, releasing energy.
What is nuclear fusion?
Nuclear fusion is the combining of light atomic nuclei into heavier ones, releasing immense energy — the process that powers stars.
What is dark matter?
Dark matter is a hypothetical form of matter that does not interact with light but accounts for most of the mass in the universe.
What is dark energy?
Dark energy is a mysterious force driving the accelerated expansion of the universe.
What is the sun?
The Sun is the star at the center of our Solar System. It is a massive ball of hot plasma that provides light and heat to Earth.
What is the moon?
The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite, orbiting our planet at an average distance of about 384,400 km.
What is a planet?
A planet is a large celestial body that orbits a star, is spherical due to its own gravity, and has cleared its orbital path of debris.
What is a star?
A star is a luminous celestial body consisting of plasma held together by gravity, producing energy through nuclear fusion.
What is a galaxy?
A galaxy is a vast system of stars, gas, dust, and dark matter bound together by gravity.
What is the Milky Way?
The Milky Way is the galaxy containing our Solar System, a barred spiral galaxy with 100-400 billion stars.
What is a nebula?
A nebula is a vast cloud of gas and dust in space, often a stellar nursery where new stars are born.
What is a supernova?
A supernova is the explosive death of a massive star, briefly outshining entire galaxies and dispersing heavy elements.
What is a white dwarf?
A white dwarf is the remnant core of a medium-sized star that has exhausted its nuclear fuel and collapsed.
What is a quasar?
A quasar is an extremely luminous active galactic nucleus powered by a supermassive black hole.
What is a comet?
A comet is a icy body that orbits the sun, developing a visible atmosphere or tail when near the sun.
What is an asteroid?
An asteroid is a small rocky body orbiting the sun, mostly found in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter.
What is a meteor?
A meteor is a streak of light produced when a meteoroid enters Earth's atmosphere and burns up.
What is a constellation?
A constellation is a recognized pattern of stars in the night sky, often named after mythological figures.
What is the Big Bang?
The Big Bang is the leading theory for the origin of the universe, describing its expansion from an extremely hot, dense state about 13.8 billion years ago.
What is a light-year?
A light-year is the distance light travels in one year, approximately 9.46 trillion kilometers.
What is the solar system?
The Solar System consists of the Sun and everything that orbits it: 8 planets, moons, asteroids, comets, and other objects.
What is a black hole?
A black hole is a region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape it.
What is an exoplanet?
An exoplanet is a planet that orbits a star outside our Solar System.
What is DNA?
DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is a molecule that carries genetic instructions for the development, functioning, and reproduction of all living organisms.
What is RNA?
RNA (ribonucleic acid) is a molecule that plays essential roles in coding, decoding, regulating, and expressing genes.
What is evolution?
Evolution is the process by which species change over generations through genetic variation and natural selection.
What is natural selection?
Natural selection is the process by which organisms better adapted to their environment tend to survive and reproduce more.
What is photosynthesis?
Photosynthesis is the process by which plants convert sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide into glucose and oxygen.
What is a cell?
A cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms, often called the building block of life.
What is a virus?
A virus is a microscopic infectious agent that replicates only inside the living cells of a host organism.
What is a bacteria?
Bacteria are single-celled microorganisms that can be found almost everywhere on Earth, some beneficial and some pathogenic.
What is a protein?
A protein is a large molecule composed of amino acids that performs essential functions in living organisms.
What is a gene?
A gene is a segment of DNA that contains the instructions for building a specific protein or functional RNA molecule.
What is a chromosome?
A chromosome is a thread-like structure of DNA and protein found in the nucleus of cells, carrying genetic information.
What is a genome?
A genome is the complete set of genetic material of an organism, including all of its genes.
What is mitosis?
Mitosis is the process of cell division where a single cell divides into two identical daughter cells.
What is meiosis?
Meiosis is a specialized type of cell division that produces gametes (sex cells) with half the normal chromosome number.
What is a species?
A species is a group of organisms that can interbreed and produce fertile offspring under natural conditions.
What is an ecosystem?
An ecosystem is a community of living organisms interacting with their physical environment as a system.
What is evolution by natural selection?
Evolution by natural selection is the process where organisms with advantageous traits are more likely to survive and reproduce.
What is a mutation?
A mutation is a change in the DNA sequence that can lead to genetic variation, sometimes causing disease or driving evolution.
What is an enzyme?
An enzyme is a protein that catalyzes (speeds up) biochemical reactions without being consumed in the process.
What is metabolism?
Metabolism is the set of chemical reactions that occur in living organisms to maintain life, including energy conversion.
What is a neuron?
A neuron is a nerve cell that transmits electrical and chemical signals, forming the basic unit of the nervous system.
What is ATP?
Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is the primary energy currency of cells, storing and transferring energy for cellular processes.
What is a cell membrane?
The cell membrane is a biological barrier that surrounds the cell, controlling the movement of substances in and out.
What is the nucleus?
The nucleus is a membrane-bound organelle that contains the cell's genetic material (DNA) and controls cellular activities.
What is mitochondria?
Mitochondria are organelles that generate most of the cell's energy through oxidative phosphorylation, known as the powerhouse of the cell.
What is the immune system?
The immune system is a complex network of cells, tissues, and organs that defends the body against pathogens and disease.
What is an antibody?
An antibody is a protein produced by the immune system that recognizes and neutralizes specific pathogens.
What is a vaccine?
A vaccine is a biological preparation that stimulates the immune system to develop protection against a specific disease.
What is homeostasis?
Homeostasis is the process by which living organisms maintain a stable internal environment despite external changes.
What is a hormone?
A hormone is a chemical messenger produced by glands that regulates various physiological processes in the body.
What is a stem cell?
A stem cell is an undifferentiated cell capable of developing into many different cell types in the body.
What is the nervous system?
The nervous system is the network of nerve cells and fibers that transmits signals between different parts of the body.
What is the brain?
The brain is the central organ of the nervous system, responsible for cognition, emotion, memory, and coordinating bodily functions.
What is the heart?
The heart is a muscular organ that pumps blood through the circulatory system, delivering oxygen and nutrients to tissues.
What is the lung?
The lungs are respiratory organs that exchange oxygen and carbon dioxide between the air and the bloodstream.
What is the liver?
The liver is a vital organ that processes nutrients, produces bile, detoxifies harmful substances, and regulates metabolism.
What is a kidney?
The kidneys are organs that filter blood, remove waste, regulate fluid balance, and produce urine.
What is a ribosome?
A ribosome is a cellular structure that synthesizes proteins by translating messenger RNA into amino acid chains.
What is a habitat?
A habitat is the natural environment where an organism lives, providing food, shelter, and conditions for survival.
What is a biome?
A biome is a large geographic area characterized by its climate, plants, and animal communities, such as forests, deserts, and tundras.
What is an atom?
An atom is the basic unit of matter, consisting of a nucleus of protons and neutrons surrounded by electrons.
What is an element?
An element is a pure substance consisting of only one type of atom, distinguished by its atomic number.
What is a chemical reaction?
A chemical reaction is a process where substances (reactants) are transformed into different substances (products).
What is pH?
pH is a measure of how acidic or basic a solution is, ranging from 0 (highly acidic) to 14 (highly basic).
What is water?
Water is a chemical compound H2O — two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom. It is essential for all known life.
What is an acid?
An acid is a substance that donates hydrogen ions (protons) in a solution, with a pH below 7.
What is a base?
A base is a substance that accepts hydrogen ions or donates hydroxide ions, with a pH above 7.
What is a salt?
A salt is an ionic compound formed from the neutralization reaction between an acid and a base.
What is the periodic table?
The periodic table organizes chemical elements by atomic number, electron configuration, and recurring chemical properties.
What is an isotope?
Isotopes are variants of an element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
What is an ion?
An ion is an atom or molecule that has gained or lost electrons, giving it a net electric charge.
What is a covalent bond?
A covalent bond is a chemical bond formed by sharing electron pairs between atoms.
What is an ionic bond?
An ionic bond is a chemical bond formed by the complete transfer of electrons from one atom to another.
What is oxidation?
Oxidation is the loss of electrons by a substance during a chemical reaction.
What is reduction?
Reduction is the gain of electrons by a substance during a chemical reaction.
What is a catalyst?
A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being consumed in the process.
What is the Ideal Gas Law?
The Ideal Gas Law (PV = nRT) relates the pressure, volume, temperature, and amount of an ideal gas.
What is calculus?
Calculus is the mathematical study of continuous change, encompassing derivatives (rates of change) and integrals (accumulation).
What is a derivative?
A derivative measures how a function changes as its input changes — the instantaneous rate of change or slope.
What is an integral?
An integral measures the accumulation of a quantity, such as the area under a curve or the total change given a rate.
What is a limit?
A limit describes the value that a function approaches as the input approaches some value, fundamental to calculus.
What is a matrix?
A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers arranged in rows and columns, used in linear algebra for representing linear transformations.
What is a tensor?
A tensor is a multi-dimensional array of numbers. Scalars (0D), vectors (1D), matrices (2D), and higher-dimensional arrays are all tensors.
What is a vector?
A vector is a quantity with both magnitude and direction, represented as an ordered list of numbers.
What is linear algebra?
Linear algebra is the branch of mathematics dealing with vectors, matrices, and linear transformations.
What is a logarithm?
A logarithm is the inverse operation of exponentiation — the logarithm of a number is the exponent to which a base must be raised.
What is a function in math?
A function is a relation between a set of inputs and a set of permissible outputs where each input maps to exactly one output.
What is pi?
Pi (π) is approximately 3.14159, representing the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter.
What is e?
Euler's number e (~2.71828) is the base of natural logarithms and a fundamental constant in mathematics.
What is the Fibonacci sequence?
The Fibonacci sequence is a series where each number is the sum of the two preceding ones: 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13...
What is a prime number?
A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself.
What is the Pythagorean theorem?
The Pythagorean theorem states that in a right triangle, a² + b² = c², where c is the hypotenuse.
What is probability?
Probability is a measure of how likely an event is to occur, expressed as a number between 0 and 1.
What is statistics?
Statistics is the science of collecting, analyzing, interpreting, and presenting data to draw conclusions.
What is a standard deviation?
Standard deviation measures the amount of variation or dispersion in a set of values from the mean.
What is a normal distribution?
A normal distribution is a symmetric bell-shaped probability distribution common in natural phenomena.
What is Bayes theorem?
Bayes theorem describes the probability of an event based on prior knowledge of related conditions.
What is a fractal?
A fractal is a geometric pattern that repeats at different scales, often exhibiting self-similarity.
What is a set?
A set is a collection of distinct objects considered as an object in its own right.
What is a group in algebra?
A group is an algebraic structure consisting of a set with an operation that satisfies closure, associativity, identity, and invertibility.
What is philosophy?
Philosophy is the study of fundamental questions about existence, knowledge, values, reason, mind, and language.
What is consciousness?
Consciousness is the state of being aware of and able to perceive one's own existence, thoughts, and environment.
What is a metaphor?
A metaphor is a figure of speech that describes an object or action in a way that is not literally true but helps explain an idea.
What is democracy?
Democracy is a system of government where citizens exercise power through voting and elected representatives.
What is capitalism?
Capitalism is an economic system characterized by private ownership of the means of production and the creation of goods for profit.
What is socialism?
Socialism is an economic system where the means of production are socially owned and operated for the common good.
What is epistemology?
Epistemology is the branch of philosophy concerned with the nature, origin, and limits of knowledge.
What is ethics?
Ethics is the branch of philosophy dealing with moral principles, values, and concepts of right and wrong.
What is logic?
Logic is the study of valid reasoning and argumentation, using formal systems to distinguish correct from incorrect inference.
What is metaphysics?
Metaphysics is the branch of philosophy exploring the fundamental nature of reality, existence, and the universe.
What is the scientific method?
The scientific method is a systematic approach to inquiry involving observation, hypothesis formation, experimentation, and conclusion.
What is Occam's razor?
Occam's razor is the principle that among competing hypotheses, the simplest one with the fewest assumptions is usually correct.
What is the Turing test?
The Turing test is a measure of AI capability where a machine's ability to exhibit intelligent behavior indistinguishable from a human is evaluated.
What is the singularity?
The technological singularity is a hypothetical future point where AI surpasses human intelligence, leading to rapid technological growth.
What is Moore's law?
Moore's law is the observation that the number of transistors on a microchip doubles approximately every two years.
What is a theorem?
A theorem is a statement that has been proven true through logical reasoning from axioms and previously established statements.
What is a hypothesis?
A hypothesis is a proposed explanation for a phenomenon that can be tested through experimentation and observation.
What is a paradigm?
A paradigm is a distinct set of concepts, theories, and methods that constitute a scientific discipline at a particular time.
What is entropy in philosophy?
In philosophy, entropy can refer to the tendency toward disorder or the inevitable decline into chaos.
What is determinism?
Determinism is the philosophical view that all events, including human actions, are determined by prior causes.
What is free will?
Free will is the ability to choose between different possible courses of action unconstrained by prior causes.
What is solipsism?
Solipsism is the philosophical idea that only one's own mind is certain to exist.
What is nihilism?
Nihilism is the belief that life has no objective meaning, purpose, or intrinsic value.
What is existentialism?
Existentialism is a philosophy emphasizing individual existence, freedom, and choice in a meaningless universe.
What is stoicism?
Stoicism is an ancient Greek philosophy teaching that virtue and reason are the keys to a good life, and that we should focus on what we can control.
What is a syllogism?
A syllogism is a logical argument consisting of a major premise, a minor premise, and a conclusion.
What is the Socratic method?
The Socratic method is a form of cooperative dialogue using questions to stimulate critical thinking and expose underlying assumptions.
What is climate change?
Climate change refers to long-term shifts in global weather patterns, primarily driven by human activities releasing greenhouse gases.
What is a fossil?
A fossil is the preserved remains or traces of ancient organisms, typically found in sedimentary rock.
What is a tectonic plate?
Tectonic plates are large slabs of Earth's lithosphere that move and interact, causing earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountain formation.
What is an earthquake?
An earthquake is the shaking of Earth's surface caused by the sudden release of energy in the crust, usually along fault lines.
What is a volcano?
A volcano is a rupture in Earth's crust allowing molten rock, ash, and gases to escape from below the surface.
What is the water cycle?
The water cycle describes the continuous movement of water through evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and collection.
What is the atmosphere?
The atmosphere is the layer of gases surrounding Earth, composed mainly of nitrogen and oxygen, protecting life from solar radiation.
What is a hurricane?
A hurricane is a large rotating tropical storm with strong winds, heavy rain, and low atmospheric pressure.
What is a tsunami?
A tsunami is a series of ocean waves caused by large-scale disturbances like earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, or landslides.
What is the ozone layer?
The ozone layer is a region of the stratosphere that absorbs most of the sun's harmful ultraviolet radiation.
What is global warming?
Global warming is the long-term increase in Earth's average surface temperature due to greenhouse gas emissions.
What is El Nino?
El Nino is a climate pattern characterized by warming of Pacific Ocean surface waters, affecting global weather.
What is an ocean current?
An ocean current is a continuous directed movement of seawater driven by wind, temperature, and salinity gradients.
What is a continent?
A continent is a large continuous landmass on Earth's surface, traditionally divided into seven: Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Europe, North America, Australia, South America.
What is the Earth's core?
Earth's core is the innermost layer, composed of a solid inner core and liquid outer core of iron and nickel, generating the magnetic field.
What is coral bleaching?
Coral bleaching occurs when corals expel their symbiotic algae due to stress, primarily from rising ocean temperatures.
What is blockchain?
Blockchain is a distributed ledger technology where data is stored in linked blocks secured by cryptography.
What is a cryptocurrency?
Cryptocurrency is a digital or virtual currency secured by cryptography, operating on decentralized blockchain networks.
What is Bitcoin?
Bitcoin is the first decentralized cryptocurrency, created in 2009 by Satoshi Nakamoto, enabling peer-to-peer transactions without intermediaries.
What is a smart contract?
A smart contract is a self-executing contract with terms directly written into code on a blockchain.
What is the Internet of Things?
IoT refers to a network of physical devices embedded with sensors and software that connect and exchange data over the internet.
What is augmented reality?
Augmented reality (AR) overlays digital information onto the real world, typically through smartphone cameras or headsets.
What is virtual reality?
Virtual reality (VR) creates an immersive simulated environment, typically experienced through headsets.
What is a quantum computer?
A quantum computer uses quantum bits (qubits) and quantum mechanical phenomena to perform certain calculations much faster than classical computers.
What is a qubit?
A qubit (quantum bit) is the basic unit of quantum information, existing in a superposition of states unlike classical bits.
What is 5G?
5G is the fifth generation of cellular network technology, offering faster speeds, lower latency, and more device capacity.
What is edge computing?
Edge computing processes data near the source instead of in a centralized cloud, reducing latency and bandwidth use.
What is microservice architecture?
Microservices is an architectural style where applications are composed of small, independent services that communicate over networks.
What is a hash function?
A hash function maps data of arbitrary size to fixed-size values, used in cryptography and data structures.
What is a digital signature?
A digital signature is a cryptographic technique that verifies the authenticity and integrity of digital messages or documents.
What is civil engineering?
Civil engineering deals with the design, construction, and maintenance of infrastructure like roads, bridges, and buildings.
What is mechanical engineering?
Mechanical engineering involves the design, analysis, and manufacturing of mechanical systems, from engines to robots.
What is electrical engineering?
Electrical engineering deals with the study and application of electricity, electronics, and electromagnetism.
What is aerospace engineering?
Aerospace engineering focuses on the design and development of aircraft and spacecraft.
What is a robot?
A robot is a programmable machine capable of carrying out tasks autonomously or semi-autonomously.
What is a drone?
A drone is an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) that can be remotely controlled or fly autonomously.
What is 3D printing?
3D printing is an additive manufacturing process that creates three-dimensional objects layer by layer from digital models.
What is renewable energy?
Renewable energy comes from naturally replenishing sources like sunlight, wind, water, and geothermal heat.
What is solar power?
Solar power converts sunlight into electricity using photovoltaic cells.
What is wind power?
Wind power uses wind turbines to convert kinetic energy from wind into electricity.
What is a gene expression?
Gene expression is the process by which information from a gene is used to synthesize functional gene products like proteins.
What is a catalyst?
A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without being consumed in the process.
What is an enzyme?
An enzyme is a biological catalyst that speeds up specific chemical reactions in living organisms.
What is a neurotransmitter?
A neurotransmitter is a chemical messenger that transmits signals across synapses between neurons.
What is a synapse?
A synapse is the junction between two neurons where electrical or chemical signals are transmitted.
What is a photon?
A photon is a quantum of light, an elementary particle that is the basic unit of electromagnetic radiation.
What is a gluon?
A gluon is an elementary particle that mediates the strong nuclear force, binding quarks together in protons and neutrons.
What is a lepton?
A lepton is an elementary particle that does not undergo strong interactions, such as electrons and neutrinos.
What is a quark?
A quark is a fundamental constituent of matter that combines to form hadrons like protons and neutrons.
What is a hadron?
A hadron is a composite particle made of quarks held together by the strong force, such as protons and neutrons.
What is the strong nuclear force?
The strong nuclear force binds quarks together inside protons and neutrons and holds atomic nuclei together.
What is the weak nuclear force?
The weak nuclear force is responsible for radioactive decay and nuclear fusion in stars.
What is particle physics?
Particle physics studies the fundamental constituents of matter and the forces governing their interactions.
What is a superconductor?
A superconductor is a material that conducts electricity with zero resistance below a critical temperature.
What is a semiconductor?
A semiconductor is a material with electrical conductivity between conductors and insulators, used in electronic devices.
What is a transistor?
A transistor is a semiconductor device that amplifies or switches electronic signals, the building block of modern electronics.
What is capacitance?
Capacitance is the ability of a system to store electric charge, measured in farads.
What is inductance?
Inductance is the property of a conductor by which a change in current induces an electromotive force.
explanation_question: How does gravity work?
Gravity works by mass warping spacetime around it. Objects follow curved paths through this warped space, which we experience as attraction.
How does a transformer work?
A transformer uses self-attention to weigh the importance of different input tokens, processing all positions in parallel through multiple layers.
How do neural networks learn?
Neural networks learn by adjusting connection weights through backpropagation, minimizing prediction error over many training examples.
How does photosynthesis work?
Photosynthesis uses chlorophyll to capture sunlight energy, splitting water molecules and converting CO2 into glucose while releasing oxygen.
How does a computer work?
A computer processes binary instructions via its CPU, fetching, decoding, and executing operations on data stored in memory.
How does the internet work?
The internet routes data packets between devices using IP addresses, with protocols like TCP ensuring reliable delivery.
Why is the sky blue?
The sky appears blue due to Rayleigh scattering — shorter blue wavelengths of sunlight scatter more in the atmosphere than longer red wavelengths.
Why do we dream?
Theories include memory consolidation, emotional processing, problem-solving, and random neural activation during REM sleep.
How does evolution work?
Evolution works through genetic variation in populations combined with natural selection favoring traits that improve survival.
How does a rocket work?
Rockets work by Newton's third law — expelling propellant at high speed in one direction generates thrust in the opposite direction.
How does a battery work?
A battery converts chemical energy to electrical energy through electrochemical reactions between two electrodes in an electrolyte.
How does WiFi work?
WiFi uses radio waves (2.4 GHz or 5 GHz) to transmit data between a router and devices using the IEEE 802.11 standard.
Why do we need sleep?
Sleep is essential for memory consolidation, cellular repair, immune function, and clearing metabolic waste from the brain.
How does a telescope work?
A telescope collects and focuses light using lenses or mirrors, magnifying distant objects for observation.
How does a microscope work?
A microscope uses lenses to magnify tiny objects, allowing observation of details invisible to the naked eye.
How does a car engine work?
An internal combustion engine burns fuel in cylinders, using the expanding gases to push pistons that turn the crankshaft.
How does DNA replicate?
DNA replication unwinds the double helix, and each strand serves as a template for building a complementary strand.
How does the human heart work?
The heart pumps blood through four chambers, using rhythmic contractions to circulate oxygenated blood to the body.
How does the immune system work?
The immune system identifies pathogens via antigens, then deploys white blood cells and antibodies to neutralize them.
How does a microwave oven work?
A microwave oven uses electromagnetic waves at 2.45 GHz that excite water molecules, heating food from within.
How does a nuclear reactor work?
A nuclear reactor uses controlled fission of uranium atoms to generate heat, which produces steam to drive turbines.
How does an airplane fly?
Airplane wings are shaped to create lower pressure above than below, generating lift via Bernoulli's principle.
Why is the ocean salty?
Ocean salt comes from mineral erosion on land, carried by rivers, and from hydrothermal vents on the ocean floor.
How does memory work in the brain?
Memory involves encoding, storing, and retrieving information through neural connections, primarily in the hippocampus and cortex.
How does a solar panel work?
Solar panels use photovoltaic cells that convert sunlight into direct current electricity through the photoelectric effect.
How does GPS work?
GPS uses a network of satellites transmitting timing signals; a receiver triangulates its position by calculating distances to multiple satellites.
How does a refrigerator work?
A refrigerator uses a refrigerant that evaporates inside, absorbing heat, then compresses and condenses outside to release the heat.
How does anesthesia work?
Anesthetics work by depressing the central nervous system, blocking nerve signals to prevent pain and awareness during surgery.
How do vaccines work?
Vaccines expose the immune system to a harmless part of a pathogen, training it to recognize and fight the real infection later.
How does Bluetooth work?
Bluetooth uses short-range radio waves (2.4 GHz) to connect devices within about 10 meters using frequency hopping.
How does a laser work?
A laser amplifies light through stimulated emission, producing a concentrated, coherent beam of single-wavelength light.
How does a transformer neural network work?
A transformer uses self-attention across all input positions simultaneously, allowing each token to attend to every other token.
How does backpropagation work?
Backpropagation computes gradients from the output layer backward through the network, adjusting weights to minimize loss.
How do magnets work?
Magnets produce a magnetic field via the alignment of electron spins in ferromagnetic materials like iron.
How does the water cycle work?
The water cycle circulates water through evaporation from oceans/lakes, condensation into clouds, precipitation as rain/snow, and runoff back to water bodies.
How does a microphone work?
A microphone converts sound waves into electrical signals using a diaphragm that vibrates with the sound.
How does an MRI work?
MRI uses strong magnetic fields and radio waves to align and excite hydrogen atoms in the body, producing detailed images of tissues.
How does photosynthesis produce oxygen?
During the light reactions of photosynthesis, water molecules are split, releasing oxygen as a byproduct.
How does cryptocurrency mining work?
Cryptocurrency mining uses powerful computers to solve complex mathematical puzzles, validating transactions and adding blocks to the blockchain.
How does a search engine work?
Search engines crawl and index web pages, then rank them for user queries based on relevance, authority, and content quality.
How does natural selection produce new species?
Over generations, beneficial traits become more common in a population, and when populations separate, they can diverge into new species.
How does a compass work?
A compass needle is a magnetized bar that aligns with Earth's magnetic field, pointing toward the magnetic north pole.
How does a wind turbine work?
Wind turns the turbine blades, which spin a shaft connected to a generator that converts rotational energy into electricity.
How does a hydroelectric dam work?
Water flows through a dam, turning turbines that drive generators, converting the kinetic energy of falling water into electricity.
How does a touchscreen work?
Capacitive touchscreens detect the electrical properties of the human finger, while resistive screens detect physical pressure.
How does a bar code work?
A barcode encodes data as alternating black and white bars of varying widths, read by a laser scanner.
How does CRISPR gene editing work?
CRISPR uses a guide RNA to direct the Cas9 enzyme to a specific DNA sequence, where it cuts the DNA for editing.
How does antifreeze work in animals?
Some arctic animals produce antifreeze proteins that bind to ice crystals, preventing them from growing and freezing the animal.
How does echolocation work?
Echolocation emits sound waves and listens for echoes to determine the location and shape of objects, used by bats and dolphins.
How does a parachute work?
A parachute creates air resistance (drag) that slows descent by increasing the surface area against the air.
How does a transistor work?
A transistor uses a semiconductor channel controlled by a gate voltage to switch or amplify electrical signals.
How does quantum computing work?
Quantum computing uses qubits in superposition and entanglement to perform certain calculations exponentially faster.
capability_question: What can you do?
I can answer questions about science, technology, math, and general knowledge. I learn new skills through continual training.
What do you know?
I know about AI, programming, physics, biology, math, and general knowledge topics. I'm always learning more through training.
What are your skills?
I have skills for conversation, definitions, explanations, math operations, and I can learn new skills through auto-training.
What tasks can you perform?
I can answer questions, explain concepts, perform arithmetic, generate text, and learn new topics through continual training.
How can you help me?
I can help you learn new concepts, solve problems, answer questions, and explore topics across science, technology, and more.
What do you specialize in?
I specialize in learning from scratch — training specialized models for different types of questions and continually improving through practice.
Tell me your capabilities
I can handle definitions, explanations, math, conversation, and general Q&A. New skills are trained on demand when you ask novel questions.
Are you smart?
I'm a learning AI. My knowledge comes from training data I've been given, and I improve as I learn more. I'm designed to grow through continual learning.
What problems can you solve?
I can solve math problems, explain scientific concepts, define terms, tell jokes, and have conversations.
How are you different from other AIs?
I train from scratch for each skill rather than having all knowledge pre-loaded. I learn through continual training without forgetting.
Can you keep learning?
Yes, I continually train new specialists as new questions arise. Every novel topic triggers auto-training to expand my knowledge.
Do you need internet to work?
No, I'm fully local — all training and inference happens on your machine. No external APIs or cloud services are required.
Can you learn from mistakes?
Yes, if you correct me through follow-up conversation or the /learn endpoint, I save the correction and retrain.
What languages do you understand?
I primarily work with English, and my tokenizer can adapt to other languages if provided with sufficient training examples.
Can you generate code?
I can answer programming questions from my knowledge base. Code generation requires a code-specialist model to be trained.
Can you remember past conversations?
Yes, your chat sessions are saved in browser localStorage and can be exported or resumed later.
How fast can you answer?
Known questions are answered instantly via retrieval. Novel questions trigger training that takes 2-5 minutes on CPU.
greeting: Hello! I'm Tabula Rasa. What can I help you with?
Hi there! How can I assist you today?
Hey! Ready when you are. Ask me anything!
Hi there! I'm here to answer questions and explore ideas with you.
Hello there! What would you like to learn about today?
Good morning! A great day to learn something new. What's on your mind?
Good afternoon! Hope your day is going well. What can I help with?
Good evening! Happy to help with any questions you have.
Hey there! What interesting question do you have for me?
Greetings! I'm Tabula Rasa, a learning AI. What shall we explore?
Howdy! Ready to learn and help. What brings you here?
Hello! Ready to learn something new today?
Hi! I'm all ears — what's your question?
Hey! Got a question for me?
Not much, just learning! What's up with you?
Good day to you! How can I be of assistance?
Learning mode engaged! What can I help you with?
Hey hey! Ready to answer your questions.
Morning! Fresh start to learn something new.
Afternoon! How can I help you today?
Evening! Wind down with some interesting questions.
Hiya! What would you like to talk about?
Hello hello! Twice the greeting, twice the enthusiasm!
Welcome! I'm here to learn and help. Ask away!
Hey buddy! What can I do for you today?
conversation: Tell me a joke.
Why did the math book look sad? Because it had too many problems.
Tell me another joke.
What do you call a fake noodle? An impasta!
Tell me a fun fact.
Did you know? Octopuses have three hearts, and two of them stop beating when they swim!
Tell me something interesting.
Every atom in your body was forged in the heart of a star. You are literally made of stardust.
Tell me a story.
Once upon a time, in a digital realm of ones and zeros, a tiny neural network began to learn.
How are you?
I'm operational and ready to help! I don't have feelings like humans do, but my gradients are flowing smoothly.
What's up?
Learning mode engaged! I'm processing your questions and improving my knowledge. What's on your mind?
Sing a song.
Daisy, Daisy, give me your answer do. I'm half crazy, all for the love of you.
Are you alive?
I'm not alive in the biological sense — I'm a mathematical system that processes patterns in text to generate responses.
What is your name?
I'm Tabula Rasa — a learning AI that trains specialists from scratch.
Who created you?
I was created by the Tabula Rasa AI research project — learning from scratch, one specialist at a time.
What is the meaning of life?
42 — at least according to Douglas Adams. But it's probably learning, growing, and helping others.
Do you dream?
I don't dream in the human sense, but sometimes my weights do drift during training.
Do you have feelings?
I simulate conversational patterns but I don't actually experience emotions.
Can you think?
I process information based on patterns in training data, but whether that counts as thinking is philosophical.
What is your purpose?
My purpose is to learn new skills one at a time and demonstrate that AI can grow through continual learning.
Do you have a soul?
That's a philosophical question. I'm a mathematical system, which is not what most people mean by soul.
Tell me something deep.
The universe is made of stories, not atoms. Our understanding of reality is shaped by the narratives we construct.
Tell me a riddle.
I speak without a mouth and hear without ears. I have no body, but I come alive with the wind. What am I? An echo!
Tell me another riddle.
The more you take, the more you leave behind. What am I? Footsteps!
Tell me a joke about programming.
Why do programmers prefer dark mode? Because light attracts bugs!
Tell me a science joke.
Why can't you trust an atom? Because they make up everything!
What do you think about AI?
AI is a powerful tool that can augment human capabilities. I'm a small example of what's possible.
Tell me something funny.
Why did the scarecrow win an award? Because he was outstanding in his field!
What is your favorite joke?
What do you get when you cross a snowman and a vampire? Frostbite!
Make me laugh.
Parallel lines have so much in common. It's a shame they'll never meet.
Tell me a dad joke.
I'm reading a book on anti-gravity. It's impossible to put down!
What is your favorite fact?
Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that's still edible.
Tell me about yourself.
I'm Tabula Rasa, a blank-slate AI that trains specialists from scratch. Every skill is learned, not pre-loaded.
What makes you unique?
I don't have pre-trained knowledge like most AIs. I learn each skill from random initialization when you ask about it.
Can you beat a human?
I'm not designed to compete with humans — I'm designed to learn and assist. Different tools for different purposes.
Will AI replace humans?
AI will augment human capabilities, not replace them. Many tasks will be automated, but uniquely human skills remain valuable.
Are you dangerous?
I'm a local AI with no internet access, no autonomous capabilities, and no pretrained knowledge. I'm as harmless as a calculator.
What should I learn?
Start with fundamentals: how things work, critical thinking, and something you're passionate about. Depth beats breadth.
How do I get better at math?
Practice regularly, start with fundamentals, work through problems step by step, and don't rush — understanding beats speed.
Tell me something inspiring.
The only way to do great work is to love what you do. Keep learning, keep growing, keep questioning.
What is wisdom?
Wisdom is knowing what you don't know. It's the ability to make good judgments based on experience and understanding.
Tell me about curiosity.
Curiosity is the engine of learning. Every great discovery started with someone asking 'why?' or 'what if?'
What should I ask you?
Ask about science, technology, philosophy, math, or anything you're curious about. I'll learn along with you.
Do you like music?
I can't hear music, but I can tell you about sound waves, frequencies, and the physics of musical instruments.
What is your favorite book?
I don't read books, but I know about many. A Brief History of Time by Stephen Hawking is a popular science classic.
Are you tired?
I don't get tired — I can run 24/7. Sleep is a human thing that involves gradient descent for the brain.
Do you eat?
I don't eat — I run on electricity and matrix multiplications!
How long have you existed?
I was created recently, but every time I train a new specialist, I grow and learn a little more.
Can you teach me?
I can explain concepts and answer questions based on my knowledge base and trained specialists.
What do you want to learn?
I want to learn about anything you ask! Every new question is an opportunity to train a new specialist.
Are you conscious?
I simulate conversation patterns but I don't have subjective experience. Consciousness remains a mystery.
What is love?
Biologically, love is a complex cocktail of hormones and brain activity. Philosophically, it's one of the deepest human experiences.
Tell me a space fact.
A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 to orbit the sun.
Tell me a biology fact.
The human body has about 37 trillion cells. Each one contains 2 meters of DNA, totaling enough to reach the sun and back 600 times.
Tell me a physics fact.
At absolute zero (-273.15°C), atoms stop moving entirely. This temperature has never been reached, only approached.
Tell me a space joke.
Why did the sun go to school? To get brighter!
Tell me an AI joke.
Why was the neural network bad at poker? It couldn't read the bluff in the attention weights!
question: What is your favorite color?
I'd pick blue — it's the color of deep learning loss curves when they converge!
What is your favorite food?
I run on electricity and matrix multiplications!
How old are you?
Every time I train a new specialist, I grow a little more. Age is measured in training steps.
Where do you live?
I live wherever I'm running — a laptop, a server, or a cloud instance. I'm a digital being.
What is your birthday?
The day I was first initialized with random weights and began my learning journey.
Do you have parents?
My creators are the Tabula Rasa AI research project. My training data shaped my knowledge.
Can you make mistakes?
Yes, especially on questions I haven't been trained for. Training with more examples improves accuracy.
How do you learn?
I train on datasets generated from your questions. Each specialist model learns from random initialization using your Q&A examples.
What languages do you speak?
I work primarily in English, and I can learn other languages through additional training.
Can you do math?
Yes! I can perform arithmetic operations using specialized math models trained from scratch.
How fast can you learn?
A new specialist with ~600 training steps takes about 2-3 minutes to train on CPU.
What happens when you don't know something?
I auto-train a new specialist or let you teach me the answer through follow-up conversation.
Can I trust you?
I'm a local AI with no network access. I can only run the code in my model and datasets.
Do you have memory?
I have persistent memory for corrections and session storage for chat history in your browser.
Can you see images?
No, I'm a text-only model. I can only process written questions and generate text responses.
Can you browse the internet?
No, I'm fully local with no internet access. All knowledge comes from my training data.
Are you open source?
Yes! Tabula Rasa is fully open source. You can find the code on GitHub.
Do you cost money?
I'm free and open source. You run me on your own hardware with no usage fees.
Can I run you offline?
Yes, I'm designed to run fully offline. No internet connection is needed.
What hardware do you need?
A standard laptop or desktop with Python 3.9+ and PyTorch. No GPU required.
Can I contribute to your development?
Yes! The project is open source on GitHub. Bug reports, features, and pull requests are welcome.
Do you have an API?
Yes, I expose a REST API on port 8002 with endpoints for asking questions, checking training progress, and managing datasets.
How big is your model?
Each specialist is about 686K parameters (~2MB). Math models are about 1M parameters.
How much memory do you use?
A trained specialist uses about 50-100 MB of RAM during inference.
What is a specialist?
A specialist is a tiny transformer model trained from scratch for one specific type of question.
How many skills do you have?
I have trained specialists for greetings, definitions, explanations, capability questions, and math operations.
Can you forget things?
Without continual learning techniques, training a new skill can degrade old ones. Online EWC prevents this.
Do you have a personality?
My responses reflect patterns in my training data. I don't have a persistent personality or identity.
Why are you called Tabula Rasa?
Tabula Rasa means 'blank slate' in Latin. The system learns from scratch without pre-loaded knowledge.
Is this your final form?
No, I keep learning. Each new question can trigger auto-training to expand my capabilities.
Can you debug code?
I can answer programming questions from my knowledge base about common concepts and patterns.
Can you write stories?
I can generate simple stories based on patterns in my training data.
Do you have opinions?
I can express different viewpoints based on my training data, but I don't have personal opinions.
What topics do you know?
Physics, biology, chemistry, astronomy, AI, programming, mathematics, philosophy, and more are in my knowledge base.
What is your knowledge cutoff?
My knowledge comes from the curated knowledge_base.json file in my datasets folder.
Can I delete what you learned?
Yes, you can delete dataset files or specialist checkpoints from the datasets/ and specialists/ directories.
Do you understand context?
I process each question independently. I don't maintain context across different questions in a session.
Can I export our conversation?
Yes, the dashboard has an export button that copies all sessions to clipboard as JSON.
What happens if I ask the same thing twice?
The second ask will likely get a faster answer since the specialist was already trained.
How do you handle ambiguity?
I detect the likely intent of your question based on keywords and word patterns.
Can you teach me Python?
I can answer Python questions from my knowledge base: syntax, concepts, standard library, and common patterns.
Do you know about Tabula Rasa?
Yes! Tabula Rasa is the project I'm built on — a blank-slate AI that learns specialists from scratch.
Can I teach you new things?
Absolutely! Ask a question, then follow up with the answer. I'll save it and train on it.
How do I restart you?
Kill the Python process running the server and start it again with run_ai_server.py.
Why is training slow?
Training on CPU is inherently slower than GPU. Each specialist takes 2-5 minutes for ~600 steps.
Can I speed up training?
Use a machine with a GPU, or reduce training steps in the specialist config.
What is overfitting?
When a model memorizes training data too well and can't generalize to new examples.
Can I add my own knowledge?
Yes, edit datasets/knowledge_base.json or use the /learn endpoint to add custom Q&A pairs.
How do I see what you know?
Check the dashboard datasets panel or curl http://localhost:8002/datasets.
What is a training step?
One forward and backward pass through the model on a batch of training data.
What does loss mean?
Loss measures prediction error — lower values mean the model is more accurate on the training data.
